sections.paragraph:
ἐπισκοτέω (s. σκοτ-entries; Hippocr., Pla. et al.; Jos., Bell. 5, 343, C. Ap. 1, 214; Ath.) gener. ‘darken, obscure’.

① to put someth. in the way so that one cannot see properly, block from seeing ἐπισκοτεῖσθαι ὑπὸ πράξεων be prevented by (these) occupations fr. seeing Hm 10, 1, 4 (cp. Philo, De Jos. 147).

② to put someth. in the way so that an object cannot be clearly discerned, to obscure (Menand., Fgm. 48 Kock and Kö.) pass. be obscured (Polyb. 2, 39, 12 ἐπισκοτεῖσθαι κ. κωλύεσθαι, of pers. whose qualifications are not recognized; Heraclit. Sto. 19 p. 29, 15) of the Spirit μὴ ἐπισκοτούμενον ὑπὸ ἑτέρου πονηροῦ πνεύματος Hm 5, 1, 2 (the imagery is one of contrast between a bright and open [καθαρόν] spirit and a base spirit that darkens; cp. Diog. L. 5, 76 ἐπεσκοτήθη ὑπὸ τοῦ φθόνου).—DELG s.v. σκότος.

Hippocr Hippocr = Corpus Hippocraticum, V–IV b.c.—List 5

Pla Pla , V–IV b.c.; s. also Ps.-Plato—List 5

et al. et al. = et alii (and others)

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

Ath. Ath. = Athenagoras, II a.d.—List 5

gener. gener. = generally

someth. someth. = something

  1. fr. = from

Hm Hm = Mandates

  1. cp. = compare, freq. in ref. to citation fr. ancient texts

Philo Philo = P. of Alexandria, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

Menand Menand , various dramas cited; IV–III b.c.—List 5

Fgm. Fgm. = fragment, fragmentary

Kock Kock , s. Com.—List 5

pass. pass. = passive (either of grammatical form or of passive experience); also used in reference to literary portion=passage

Polyb Polyb , III–II b.c.—List 5

pers. pers. = person(s)

Heraclit. Sto. Heraclit. Sto. = Heraclitus the Stoic, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

Diog. L. Diog. L. = Diogenes Laertius, III a.d.—List 5

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

s.v. s.v. = sub voce (under the word, look up the word)

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 380
